subtopics.title: RNN (Vanilla RNN)
subtopics.definition: Neural network for processing sequential data
subtopics.explanation: RNNs process sequences by maintaining a hidden state that captures information from previous time steps. At each step, hidden state h_t = tanh(W_hh·h_{t-1} + W_xh·x_t + b). RNNs share weights across time steps, enabling them to handle variable-length sequences. Applications: text generation, sentiment analysis, time series prediction, speech recognition. Limitations: vanishing/exploding gradients, difficulty capturing long-range dependencies (>10-20 steps). Architectures: many-to-one (sentiment), one-to-many (text generation), many-to-many (machine translation).
subtopics.example: Sentence 'The cat sat on the mat' processed word by word. Hidden state at 'sat' encodes context of 'The cat'. At 'mat', state contains entire sentence meaning. RNN predicts next word.
subtopics.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import numpy as np

class SimpleRNN(nn.Module):
    """Manual RNN implementation"""
    
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, output_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_size = hidden_size
        self.W_xh = nn.Linear(input_size, hidden_size)
        self.W_hh = nn.Linear(hidden_size, hidden_size)
        self.W_hy = nn.Linear(hidden_size, output_size)
        self.tanh = nn.Tanh()
    
    def forward(self, x, hidden=None):
        """x: (seq_len, batch, input_size)"""
        seq_len, batch_size, _ = x.shape
        if hidden is None:
            hidden = torch.zeros(batch_size, self.hidden_size).to(x.device)
        
        outputs = []
        for t in range(seq_len):
            hidden = self.tanh(self.W_xh(x[t]) + self.W_hh(hidden))
            outputs.append(self.W_hy(hidden))
        
        return torch.stack(outputs), hidden

class PyTorchRNN(nn.Module):
    """PyTorch RNN with multiple layers"""
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, output_size, dropout=0.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.rnn = nn.RNN(input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, batch_first=True, dropout=dropout)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_size, output_size)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        out, hidden = self.rnn(x)
        return self.fc(out), hidden

print("🔷 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)")
print("=" * 60)

rnn = SimpleRNN(input_size=10, hidden_size=20, output_size=5)
x = torch.randn(3, 2, 10)  # (seq_len=3, batch=2, input=10)
outputs, hidden = rnn(x)
print(f"\n📊 RNN Forward Pass:")
print(f"  Input shape: {x.shape}")
print(f"  Output shape: {outputs.shape}")
print(f"  Final hidden shape: {hidden.shape}")

print("\n✅ RNN Architectures:")
print("  • Many-to-One: Sentiment analysis (sequence → label)")
print("  • One-to-Many: Text generation (label → sequence)")
print("  • Many-to-Many: Machine translation (seq → seq)")
print("  • Time series forecasting")
print("\n⚠️ RNN Limitations:")
print("  • Vanishing/exploding gradients")
print("  • Cannot learn long-range dependencies (>20 steps)")
print("  • Sequential processing (can't parallelize)")

subtopics.title: Vanishing Gradient Problem
subtopics.definition: Gradients become extremely small during backpropagation
subtopics.explanation: The vanishing gradient problem occurs when gradients become exponentially small as they propagate backward through time (BPTT). For RNNs, gradients are multiplied by the weight matrix at each step. If eigenvalues < 1, gradients vanish; if > 1, gradients explode. Vanishing gradients prevent RNNs from learning long-range dependencies (>10-20 steps). Solutions: 1) LSTM/GRU (gated architectures), 2) Gradient clipping (for exploding), 3) Skip connections, 4) Proper initialization (Xavier/He), 5) ReLU activations.
subtopics.example: Sentence 'The man who lived in Paris for 20 years speaks fluent French.' RNN needs to link 'man' → 'speaks' across 10+ words. Vanishing gradient makes this connection impossible.
subtopics.code:
print("\n🔷 Vanishing Gradient Problem")
print("=" * 60)

class GradientDemo:
    @staticmethod
    def gradient_magnitude(seq_len, weight):
        """Simulate gradient magnitude after backprop through time"""
        return weight ** seq_len

print("\n📊 Gradient Magnitude vs Sequence Length:")
print(f"{'Steps':<10} {'w=0.5':<15} {'w=0.9':<15} {'w=1.1':<15}")
print("-" * 55)
for steps in [1, 5, 10, 20, 50]:
    vanish = GradientDemo.gradient_magnitude(steps, 0.5)
    stable = GradientDemo.gradient_magnitude(steps, 0.9)
    explode = GradientDemo.gradient_magnitude(steps, 1.1)
    print(f"{steps:<10} {vanish:<15.2e} {stable:<15.2e} {explode:<15.2e}")

print("\n✅ Solutions:")
print("  • LSTM/GRU: Gated architectures (most effective)")
print("  • Gradient Clipping: Cap gradients at threshold")
print("  • Residual Connections: Skip connections")
print("  • Proper Initialization: Xavier/He initialization")
print("  • ReLU Activations: Avoid saturation")

subtopics.title: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)
subtopics.definition: Advanced RNN with gated memory cell
subtopics.explanation: LSTM solves vanishing gradient using gated memory cells with three gates: 1) Forget gate (f_t): decides what to discard, 2) Input gate (i_t): decides what to store, 3) Output gate (o_t): decides what to output. Cell state (c_t) carries long-term memory. LSTM can learn dependencies over hundreds of steps. Equations: f_t = σ(W_f·[h_{t-1},x_t]), i_t = σ(W_i·[h_{t-1},x_t]), c̃_t = tanh(W_c·[h_{t-1},x_t]), c_t = f_t*c_{t-1} + i_t*c̃_t, o_t = σ(W_o·[h_{t-1},x_t]), h_t = o_t*tanh(c_t).
subtopics.example: LSTM reading a book: forget gate drops irrelevant info, input gate adds new important facts, output gate produces answers based on memory. Can remember character names across chapters.
subtopics.code:
class LSTMCell(nn.Module):
    """Manual LSTM cell implementation"""
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_size = hidden_size
        self.W_ih = nn.Linear(input_size, 4 * hidden_size)
        self.W_hh = nn.Linear(hidden_size, 4 * hidden_size)
    
    def forward(self, x, state=None):
        batch_size = x.size(0)
        if state is None:
            h = torch.zeros(batch_size, self.hidden_size).to(x.device)
            c = torch.zeros(batch_size, self.hidden_size).to(x.device)
        else:
            h, c = state
        
        gates = self.W_ih(x) + self.W_hh(h)
        i, f, g, o = gates.chunk(4, dim=1)
        
        i = torch.sigmoid(i)
        f = torch.sigmoid(f)
        g = torch.tanh(g)
        o = torch.sigmoid(o)
        
        c = f * c + i * g
        h = o * torch.tanh(c)
        return h, c

class PyTorchLSTM(nn.Module):
    """PyTorch LSTM with multiple layers"""
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, output_size, dropout=0.0, bidirectional=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, batch_first=True, dropout=dropout, bidirectional=bidirectional)
        direction = 2 if bidirectional else 1
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_size * direction, output_size)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        out, (hidden, cell) = self.lstm(x)
        return self.fc(out), hidden, cell

print("\n🔷 LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)")
print("=" * 60)

lstm_cell = LSTMCell(input_size=10, hidden_size=64)
x = torch.randn(4, 5, 10)  # (batch=4, seq=5, input=10)
h, c = None, None
for t in range(5):
    h, c = lstm_cell(x[:, t, :], (h, c))
print(f"\n📊 LSTM Output:")
print(f"  Hidden shape: {h.shape}, Cell shape: {c.shape}")
print(f"  Parameters: {sum(p.numel() for p in lstm_cell.parameters()):,}")

print("\n📊 LSTM vs RNN:")
print("  Feature           | RNN        | LSTM")
print("  ------------------|------------|------------------")
print("  Memory duration   | Short (~10) | Long (>100)")
print("  Vanishing gradient| Severe     | Solved")
print("  Parameters        | Fewer      | More (4x gates)")
print("  Long dependencies | Poor       | Excellent")

subtopics.title: GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)
subtopics.explanation: GRU simplifies LSTM by combining forget and input gates into a single update gate (z_t) and using reset gate (r_t). GRU has no separate cell state; hidden state (h_t) serves as memory. Equations: z_t = σ(W_z·[h_{t-1},x_t]), r_t = σ(W_r·[h_{t-1},x_t]), h̃_t = tanh(W_h·[r_t*h_{t-1},x_t]), h_t = (1-z_t)*h_{t-1} + z_t*h̃_t. GRU has fewer parameters than LSTM (3 gates vs 4), faster training, similar performance. Often preferred for smaller datasets and faster inference.
subtopics.example: GRU for text classification: update gate decides how much to keep from previous state, reset gate decides how much to forget. Works well with 25-50% fewer parameters than LSTM.
subtopics.code:
class GRUCell(nn.Module):
    """Manual GRU cell implementation"""
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_size = hidden_size
        self.W_z = nn.Linear(input_size + hidden_size, hidden_size)
        self.W_r = nn.Linear(input_size + hidden_size, hidden_size)
        self.W_h = nn.Linear(input_size + hidden_size, hidden_size)
    
    def forward(self, x, h=None):
        batch_size = x.size(0)
        if h is None:
            h = torch.zeros(batch_size, self.hidden_size).to(x.device)
        
        combined = torch.cat([x, h], dim=1)
        z = torch.sigmoid(self.W_z(combined))
        r = torch.sigmoid(self.W_r(combined))
        
        combined_reset = torch.cat([x, r * h], dim=1)
        h_tilde = torch.tanh(self.W_h(combined_reset))
        
        h_new = (1 - z) * h + z * h_tilde
        return h_new

class PyTorchGRU(nn.Module):
    """PyTorch GRU with multiple layers"""
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, output_size, dropout=0.0, bidirectional=False):
        super().__init__()
        self.gru = nn.GRU(input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, batch_first=True, dropout=dropout, bidirectional=bidirectional)
        direction = 2 if bidirectional else 1
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_size * direction, output_size)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        out, hidden = self.gru(x)
        return self.fc(out), hidden

print("\n🔷 GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)")
print("=" * 60)

gru_cell = GRUCell(input_size=10, hidden_size=64)
x = torch.randn(4, 5, 10)
h = None
for t in range(5):
    h = gru_cell(x[:, t, :], h)
print(f"\n📊 GRU Output:")
print(f"  Hidden shape: {h.shape}")
print(f"  Parameters: {sum(p.numel() for p in gru_cell.parameters()):,}")

print("\n📊 GRU vs LSTM:")
print("  Feature           | LSTM           | GRU")
print("  ------------------|----------------|------------------")
print("  Gates             | 3 (input, forget, output) | 2 (update, reset)")
print("  Cell state        | Yes (c_t)      | No (h_t only)")
print("  Parameters        | More (4x)      | Less (3x)")
print("  Training speed    | Slower         | Faster")
print("  Memory usage      | Higher         | Lower")
print("  Performance       | Slightly better| Comparable")

print("\n📊 RNN Family Comparison:")
print("-" * 60)
print("  Model | Gates | Memory | Speed | Long Dep | Best For")
print("  ------|-------|--------|-------|----------|------------------")
print("  RNN   | 0     | Poor   | Fast  | No       | Short sequences")
print("  GRU   | 2     | Good   | Fast  | Yes      | Small datasets")
print("  LSTM  | 3     | Best   | Slow  | Yes      | Long sequences")

print("\n💡 Selection Guide:")
print("  • Small dataset → GRU (less overfitting)")
print("  • Long sequences (>100 steps) → LSTM")
print("  • Fast inference needed → GRU")
print("  • State-of-the-art → LSTM or Transformer")
print("  • Need context from both directions → Bidirectional LSTM/GRU")
print("  • Stacked layers for complexity → Multi-layer LSTM/GRU")
